content:
10 min read
4 hours ago

--

Written against the GA announcement of 16 July 2026. The before-and-after code in the migration section is reproduced from Salesforce’s release post, linked at the bottom. If you built during the beta, skip to “Five things to change.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve been asking the same question about Salesforce for years. Can I build this in React?
The answer was always no, or at least not properly. You could hide a React app behind a Visualforce page. You could ship a bundle as a static resource and hope nothing in it needed the platform. You could host it on Vercel and talk to the org over the API like any other outside system, at which point you were responsible for external hosting, OAuth and session integration, and much more of the application’s security boundary, and wondering what exactly you were paying Salesforce for.
Multi-Framework went into open beta at TDX in April. On 16 July it went GA.
The GA release adds production support and removes the old beta opt-in. Your org still has to meet the documented edition, hosting, language and Salesforce App Domain requirements, so “no opt-in” is not the same as “it’s already on.” More on that below.
It also arrived with breaking changes, and one of the failure modes doesn’t look like a failure at all.
The short version of what it is
A framework-agnostic runtime on the Headless 360 Platform. React today, Angular next.
The word that matters is natively. Your app isn’t in an iframe borrowing a session. Salesforce serves it and runs it inside the authenticated org context. When you reach data through the supported platform APIs, those APIs apply the relevant Salesforce security and governance controls, and your application code never has to acquire, store or refresh an access token.
Worth being precise about that, because it’s easy to over-read. The enforcement lives in the API path you use, not in the fact that your code is running on Salesforce. GraphQL and UI API, REST endpoints and Apex REST don’t all behave identically, and the current data guidance draws distinctions between them. Nothing about the runtime makes arbitrary client code safe.
Still, it’s a different world from what came before, where I was rebuilding parts of the authentication and integration boundary myself and getting them subtly wrong.
The new domain
Employee-facing apps now live on a dedicated origin, salesforce.app. URLs look like this:
https://<org>--<namespace>.<instance>.my.salesforce.app/app/c__<bundleName>I skimmed past this the first time as a cosmetic change. It isn’t. Every app gets its own origin, which means the browser’s Same Origin Policy handles isolation for free. One app can’t read another’s cookies or storage. No proprietary sandbox layer, no custom rules to get wrong.
It’s the kind of decision that reads as a footnote in a release note and quietly removes a whole category of bug.
To check whether your org has it, go to Setup and find React Development with Salesforce Multi-Framework. An enabled org shows an “About Salesforce Multi-Framework” panel with the Enable Salesforce App Domain toggle switched on.
How a bundle actually gets on screen
The unit of work is a UI Bundle, a metadata type inside a normal DX project. Inside it sits a React project that would look entirely unremarkable to someone who’s never opened Salesforce. The generated template and Salesforce’s recipes project both build with Vite. The CLI ships a handful of React templates — a bare reactbasic, plus internal-facing and external-facing variants that scaffold the extra metadata each case needs — and React is the only framework supported today, even though the UI Bundle model is deliberately built to take others later.
There’s also a ui-bundle.json holding the bundle's runtime configuration. For a React Router app the setting that matters is routing.fallback: point it at index.html so that a refresh on a sub-route like /accounts serves the app rather than a 404. The current guide documents the whole routing block, including rewrite and redirect rules, if you need more than the fallback.
What definitely changed at GA is how the bundle surfaces.
During the beta, a bundle targeted AppLauncher and rendered from there. That target is deprecated. Employee apps now declare CustomApplication in .uibundle-meta.xml, and the Experience target for customer-facing apps stays as it was.
But the target alone does nothing. You also need a Custom Application pointing back at the bundle via a <uiBundle> reference, and a permission set granting visibility to that application. Three files, not one.
This is the part worth slowing down for. The deployment can succeed while the application stays absent from the App Launcher for any user who hasn’t been granted application visibility. No error, no warning, nothing in the CLI output pointing at the cause. Salesforce’s recipes repo keeps permission set assignment as an explicit step after deployment, which I now read as a warning rather than a formality.
The data layer, and the rename
Here’s the real shift, and it predates GA.
An LWC developer reaches for @wire. You declare what you want, the framework hands it over, and Lightning Data Service quietly handles caching and change propagation across the page. Good abstraction. You stop noticing it after a month.
React bundles don’t use Lightning Data Service or @wire. You assemble the data lifecycle yourself out of the SDK, React state and effects, subscriptions where they fit, and whatever caching layer you decide you want. Reactive updates are available through QueryResult.subscribe, so this isn't a bare-metal situation, but the composition is your problem now.
The client library itself changed name and shape at GA. @salesforce/sdk-data became @salesforce/platform-sdk. Reads and writes used to share one generic graphql() call; now reads go through .query() with a query key and writes through .mutate() with a mutation key. Response typing tightened too, so result.data is modelled as possibly undefined and you optional-chain through it.
Salesforce’s release post shows both sides. Beta:
import { createDataSDK, gql } from '@salesforce/sdk-data';const sdk = await createDataSDK();const result = await sdk.graphql?.<QueryResponse>({ query: MY_QUERY });
const mutationResult = await sdk.graphql?.<MutationResponse>({
query: MY_MUTATION,
variables: { input },
});const edges = result?.data.uiapi?.query?.Account?.edges;GA:
import { createDataSDK, gql } from '@salesforce/platform-sdk';const sdk = await createDataSDK();const result = await sdk.graphql?.query<QueryResponse>({ query: MY_QUERY });
const mutationResult = await sdk.graphql?.mutate<MutationResponse>({
mutation: MY_MUTATION,
variables: { input },
});const edges = result?.data?.uiapi?.query?.Account?.edges;One caveat before you copy either block. These imports reproduce the announcement as published on 16 July, which uses the bare @salesforce/platform-sdk specifier. The current Data SDK reference pages import from @salesforce/platform-sdk/data instead, and even the recipes repo mixes the two, so treat /data as the current form and confirm against the exports actually installed in your package.json before wiring this in. Treat the samples above as the shape of the change rather than as a snippet to paste.
One more thing worth knowing here. Multi-Framework itself is GA, but the current developer guide still labels the Data SDK documentation as beta, so check the support status of the specific SDK surface you’re planning to depend on rather than assuming GA propagates down to every subordinate piece.
Your GraphQL documents don’t change either way. Only the client wrapping them moved.
The responses nest deeper than anyone expects, because field values come back as envelopes rather than plain scalars: each is an object carrying a value (the raw value, which you use for logic and for writing back) and a displayValue (the locale-formatted string, which you use for rendering). Name isn't a string. Name.value is. You adjust, but the first afternoon involves a lot of console.log. The recipes repo has a schema fetch and codegen step that's worth lifting if you'd rather have typed queries than hand-written strings.
Five things to change
If you built during the beta, you need to update the bundle before relying on it under GA. Depending on what your old metadata and SDK usage looked like, you may hit build errors, deployment errors, or an app that deploys cleanly and can’t be found.
Start with the metadata, because that’s the silent one. Swap <target>AppLauncher</target> for <target>CustomApplication</target>, add the Custom Application file with its <uiBundle> reference, and add the permission set granting visibility. Then delete the UiBundleSettings block from your scratch org config, which is deprecated.
The code changes your compiler will mostly catch. Change the import to @salesforce/platform-sdk/data, subject to the caveat above. Split every graphql() call into .query() or .mutate(), watching the parameter key, because writes take mutation rather than query and a mechanical find-and-replace will miss it. Then optional-chain through result?.data, which TypeScript will nag you about once the types update.
Salesforce lists these in a different order. I’ve put metadata first because a type error stops your build in about four seconds, whereas a missing permission set produces a green deploy and an app nobody can find.
What doesn’t change
More than I expected. React behaves like React. Hooks, routing, TypeScript, npm packages including ones that would have been miserable to manage as static resources.
If your team maintains a component library it’s built up over years, that’s the actual value here. You bring it across roughly intact instead of reimplementing every piece of it in a framework used primarily within the Salesforce ecosystem.
There’s a staffing consequence buried in that. A front-end developer joining a Salesforce project used to face a framework to learn before they could write anything useful, and that cost shaped hiring, not just architecture. Take it away and the pool of people who can contribute gets a lot wider. Over a few years I think that matters more than any single feature in the release.
One caution, though. The platform handling authentication for you isn’t the same as the platform handling security for you. LWC carries protections that come from the framework itself. In a React bundle, ordinary front-end security and the correctness of your API calls are on you. The per-app origin helps. It isolates; it doesn’t absolve.
React or LWC
Both, and the line between them is clearer than the discourse suggests.
LWC stays the answer when you’re extending the Salesforce UI rather than replacing it. Record page components, list view actions, utility bar items, anything an admin needs to drop into place through App Builder. For small Salesforce-native components, LWC also avoids adding a React runtime and integrates directly with Salesforce’s component model. Someone asks you for a custom component on the Opportunity page next week, you reach for LWC and you don’t think about it.
React earns its place when you’re building a whole application. Internal dashboards, ops workspaces, portals, anything with its own navigation and its own identity as a product. More so if you already maintain React elsewhere, or need an npm package with no platform equivalent.
“React versus LWC” was always the wrong frame. Different questions, different answers.
What GA doesn’t include, and what to verify
The roadmap is a useful map of the gaps.
Microfrontends aren’t fully here yet. Embedding React components inside Lightning pages next to LWCs, passing events between them, is what a lot of teams are actually waiting for, and Salesforce currently labels it a developer preview rather than a released capability. If your architecture depends on React islands inside existing record pages, verify where that preview stands
